coredata.dc:title: Super-anticorrosion epoxy nanocomposites prepared with P-doped carbon dots functionalized large-size boron nitride nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Hongran
Ding, Jiheng
Wang, Hao
Miah, Mohammad Raza
Chu, Qinchao
Wang, Jinggang
Zhu, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Large-size ultrathin 2D nanosheets show huge advantages in improving the barrier and anticorrosion properties of organic coatings. However, the irreversible agglomeration of large-size nanosheets has become a key scientific issue restricting their practical applications. Herein the in situ phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs) are synthesized and decorated on large-size boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs) matrix, to empower excellent dispersion of BNNSs (stable for a few weeks) and superlative nanosheet-matrix bonding with matrix. When served as the functional fillers for epoxy (EP) coatings, the resultant PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating shows greatly enhanced corrosion resistance due to the long-range ordered self-alignment of large-size PCD@BNNSs and strong interfacial bind with coating matrix. Typically, after 5 weeks of exposure in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq), the coating resistance of the PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating maintained 6.3 × 109 O cm2, 2 ∼ 3 orders of magnitude higher than those of unmodified BNNS/EP composite (8.0 × 107 O cm2) and blank (4.2 × 106 O cm2) coatings, revealing exceptional long-term anticorrosion performance. The anticorrosion mechanism was unlocked in detail by the chemical reactions at the coating-metal interfaces, owing to the arrested incursion of aggressive species through the multi-effect synergies of superior shielding property and passivation function by the intercalated large-size and self-aligned PCD@BNNSs. Our work introduces a unique binary hybrid material system to construct high-performance coatings for future marine and aviation protection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Large-size ultrathin 2D nanosheets show huge advantages in improving the barrier and anticorrosion properties of organic coatings. However, the irreversible agglomeration of large-size nanosheets has become a key scientific issue restricting their practical applications. Herein the in situ phosphorus-doped carbon dots (PCDs) are synthesized and decorated on large-size boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs) matrix, to empower excellent dispersion of BNNSs (stable for a few weeks) and superlative nanosheet-matrix bonding with matrix. When served as the functional fillers for epoxy (EP) coatings, the resultant PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating shows greatly enhanced corrosion resistance due to the long-range ordered self-alignment of large-size PCD@BNNSs and strong interfacial bind with coating matrix. Typically, after 5 weeks of exposure in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq), the coating resistance of the PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating maintained 6.3 × 109 O cm2, 2 ∼ 3 orders of magnitude higher than those of unmodified BNNS/EP composite (8.0 × 107 O cm2) and blank (4.2 × 106 O cm2) coatings, revealing exceptional long-term anticorrosion performance. The anticorrosion mechanism was unlocked in detail by the chemical reactions at the coating-metal interfaces, owing to the arrested incursion of aggressive species through the multi-effect synergies of superior shielding property and passivation function by the intercalated large-size and self-aligned PCD@BNNSs. Our work introduces a unique binary hybrid material system to construct high-performance coatings for future marine and aviation protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic corrosion is one of the rifest challenges faced by the steel industry. Relevant researches show that the global economic losses caused by metal corrosion each year are as high as $2.5 trillion [1]. Therefore, effective technologies are urgently needed to inhibit or/and slow down the metallic corrosion. Organic coating (OC) technology demonstrates several competitive advantages when compared with other anticorrosion strategies, involving facile process, cost-effectiveness, and high protection efficiency [2]. To realize these preponderances, exploring high-performance OCs is deemed a strategic region of focus. Recently, organic composite coatings (OCCs) reinforced by 2D materials like graphene present promising, as sheet-like graphene can provide superior physical shielding property, owing to its large specific surface areas and complete impermeability [3–5]. Unfortunately, the intrinsic electrically conductive feature of graphene restricts its actual applications in metallic corrosion protection fields [6,7].

Instead, hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), an isoelectronic analogue of graphene, has been extensively concerned, due to the excellent barrier property and electrical insulation characteristics [8,9]. To date, the surface/interface modulations of hBN nanosheets (BNNSs) in OCs matrix have been widely investigated to fully utilize BNNSs for improving the corrosion resistance of OCCs [10–13]. In addition, large-size ultrathin BNNSs are highly self-alignment nature, enabling them to be readily constructed into laminated OCCs with high possible shielding function [14]. This structure can significantly block fast osmosis of aggressive species within longer diffusivity distance, which are used as elongated 2D nanochannels, thus realizing outstanding physical barrier effects [15]. Recently, Zhao et al. [16] employed large-size ultrathin BNNSs with an aspect ratio of 1240 to prepare BNNSs/epoxy (EP) composite coatings. They revealed that the protection efficiency of BNNSs/EP was 99.9% with an extremely low filler content of 0.1 wt%. Also, such BNNSs endowed the OCCs with huge versatility for various coatings applications. Although the large-size ultrathin BNNSs have significant advantages in improving the barrier and anticorrosion performance of OCCs, however, they also face the issue of agglomeration. The strong interlayer interactions of large-size BNNSs and immanent incompatibility with polymers cause inferior dispersion and slack interfacial binds, which enormously restricts the further enhancements of OCCs’ performances.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent liquid solubility and environmentally friendly, have been developed as a promising dispersant and interfacial modifierfor 2D materials [17,18]. Such CDs have π-π conjugated architecture and rich functional groups, which can facilitate the π-π interactions with BNNSs and profoundly increase effective dispersion [19]. Cui et al. [20] and Wang et al. [21] indicated that the heteroatoms like O and N on the CDs could form stable complex through coordinate on steel surfaces. It is reported that the charged sites on CDS’ surfaces can boost the O2 adsorption and O-O bonds weaking. In addition, the small-size CDs can fill the micropores and air voids of OCs’ matrix, and enhance the cross-link density and compactness of the coating matrix [22]. These are favorable for the anticorrosion performance improvement of OCCs. In this study, we synthesize the phosphorus-doped CDs (PCDs)@BNNSs composites to enhance the dispersion stability of large-size BNNSs, and induce two more efficient strategies to maximize the anticorrosion capacity of PCD@BNNS/EP composite coatings: (i) superlarge lateral size of BNNSs, and (ii) superior passivation function of PCDs. The resultant PCD@BNNS/EP coating showed outstanding long-term anticorrosion performance with high coating resistance of 6.3 × 109 O cm2 after immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 5 weeks, which is 2 ∼ 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of unmodified BNNS/EP composite (8.0 × 107 O cm2) and blank (4.2 × 106 O cm2) coatings. Based on the electrochemical, SEM and Raman tests, the protection mechanism behind PCD@BNNSs/EP coating was benefited primarily from the length and tortuosity of the 2D nanochannels and the extra passivation protection effects. Our approach exhibits the advantages for achieving the high-efficiency dispersion of large-size nanosheets, and uses it to construct OCCs with long-term corrosion resistance.

Commercial hBN (average lateral size ≈ 20 μm, 99.9% purity) powders were provided by Qingdao Tianyuan Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Polylactic acid (PLA), aminosalicylic acid (AA), and phosphoric acid (PA, 85 wt%) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Two-component waterborne EP coatings (50 wt% solid content) were provided from Zhejiang Jianhua New Materials Co., Ltd. Q235 steels (exposure area 1 cm × 1 cm) were provided by Shengxin Technology Co., Ltd (Binzhou, China). All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

Large-size ultrathin BNNSs were prepared according to our previous work [16]. The detailed process was demonstrated in Fig. S1. Typically, 2 g of bulk hBN powder was dispersed in 50 ml EtOH solution with 90 wt% PLA. The system was treated at 800 rpm for 30 min to obtain uniform suspension. Then, transfer into a 250 ml of ZrO2 tank in a planetary ball mill (XOM-2, Changsha Tianchuang Powder Technology, China) equipment. Subsequently, the hBN was ball milled for 12 h at ca. 250 rpm. Next, the samples was filtered and washed with EtOH, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 30 min to remove the un-exfoliated materials. Finally, the samples were dried at 40 °C in a vacuum oven to obtain large-size ultrathin BNNSs (Fig. S2).

For the preparation of PCDs, the CDs were firstly synthesized by our previous work [17]. The detailed process was demonstrated in Fig. S3. Typically, a certain amount of phosphoric acid (2, 5, 10 ml) was added into 50 ml CDs suspensions. The suspensions were treated by using a sonicator for 1 h, and then transferred into 50 ml PTFE autoclaves and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the black solid was collected and washed by distilled water (DIW). Finally, the product was dried under vacuum at 30 °C for 48 h to obtain PCDs (Fig. S4).

In a typical experiment, 50 mg of PCDs and 500 mg of BNNSs were added into 250 ml DIW, the mixture was then stirred 12 h at room temperature. Next, the suspension was filtered and washed by DIW to remove the free CDs. The filter cake was fired under vacuum at 30 °C for 48 h to obtain PCD@BNNSs hybrids.

PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating was fabricated by a spraying process, the filler content was set 0.5 wt%. Typically, 5 ml of PCD@BNNSs EtOH dispersion and 10 g of EP solution were mixed and stirred 30 min at 1000 rpm. Next, 8.5 g of curing agent was added into and stirred for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous liquid coating. The liquid coating was covered on polished (by sad paper from 500 to 1500 mesh) Q235 steel surfaces by an air spray gun, followed by drying for 48 h at room temperature. The coating thickness of the dried films was 30 ± 2 μm tested by thickness gauge. In control experiments, the unmodified BNNS/EP composite and pure EP coatings were also prepared in the same way, and the filler content of BNNSs was also 0.5 wt%.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI quanta 250, SE images), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM2100), atomic force microscope (AFM, VeecoMultiMode/NanoScope IIIa), Laser confocal microscope (LCM) were used to analysis surface and interface structures. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos Analytical, UK), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Discover/GADDS, Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at a scan rate of 5°/min in the range 5 to 80°, UV − visible (UV − vis) Spectrophotometer (Lambda 950 UV − vis), and Raman spectrometer (Renishaw plc, Wotton-under-edger, UK) with a laser wavelength of 532 nm were used to analysis the chemical structures and compositions. Oxygen transmittance rate (OTR, 23 °C and 50% RH) and water vapor transmittance rate (WVTR, 38 °C and 90% RH) were obtained by using Labthink VAC-V2 gas permeability tester according to GB/T 1038–2000. The corrosion behaviors of different coatings protected Q235 steel electrodes were investigated by using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai). The tests were performed with a typical three electrode system in a 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq). The coating-steel electrode (1 × 1 cm2 area) acted as working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode, and a platinum piece (1 × 1 cm2 area) as a counter-electrode. Open circuit potential (OCP) plots were scanned for 200 s to obtain stable worked status before Tafel polarization curve and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed. The Tafel plots were recorded at the polarization potential ± 200 mV around OCP in a direct current signal, and the scan rate was set 0.2 mV/s. The EIS plots were scanned from 105 ∼ 10-2 Hz after 1 day and 1–5 weeks of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq).

The absorption stabilization mechanisms of CDs, NCDs (N-doped carbon dots), PCDs on the surface of BNNSs were systematically investigated through density functional theory (DFT) calculations. First, all the molecules and hBN (001) layer were constructed and geometrically optimized using the specific module. The BNNS single layer was modeled using 10 × 10 supercells. The vacuum slam was set as 15 Å to avoid any nonbonding interactions between two adjacent layers. The carbon dots were modeled using 2 × 2 supercells, and with one –COOH group and two –OH for CDs, two –NH2 for NCDs, and two -PO3H2 for PCDs, respectively. Then, in treating the adsorption simulation, the carbon dots were randomly dispersed over the BNNS layer. The optimal configuration and energy calculation were determined by carrying out a specific module on the given force filed of the molecule within the surface region. The max adsorption distance was set as 5 Å. The results were calculated by the following equation:

are the binding energy, total internal energy, the binding energy of BNNSs, and the binding energy of PCDs, respectively.

Similar to CDs, the as-synthesized PCDs contain π-π conjugated structures, which can be easily adsorbed on BNNSs’ surfaces through strong π-πstacking interactions, in Fig. 1 (a). Binding energy simulation between one PCD and the single-layer BNNS was used to understand the absorption mechanism (Fig. 1(b) and Fig. S5), the detailed process and parameters were listed in Table S1. It is noteworthy that the binding energy of the two adjacent layers of BNNS is 0.43 eV, while the binding energy between PCD and BNNS is as high as 1.86 eV. According to this result, the adsorption structure of PCD@BNNSs is more stable than that of BNNSs themselves. This is a result of the superior bind forces from the CDs-inspired chemistry through multiple π-πstacking interactions. In view of the abundant hydrophilic hydroxyl, carboxyl and phosphate motifs, thus, the PCDs can effectively improve the dispersibility of BNNSs and restrict their re-stacking and aggregation phenomenons. It is reported that the CDs and NCDs have been applied to stabilize graphene materials in liquids based on similarπ-πstacking effects [19,20]. In Fig. 1(c), the binding energy of CD and NCD on a BNNS are 1.53 eV and 1.54 eV, much lower than that of PCD (see Fig. S5). As a result, PCDs can act unparalleled candidates to effectively stabilize and increase the dispersion capability of large-size BNNSs.

Fig. 1(d) shows the UV–vis spectra of BNNSs, PCDs, and PCD@BNNSs water solutions. The BNNSs and PCDs exhibit their characteristic absorptions at 206 nm and 258 nm, respectively. It is noted that the characteristic peak of BNNSs shifts to 207 nm, respectively, while the characteristic peak of PCDs shifts to 248 nm, for the PCD@BNNSs hybrids. This suggests that the existence of electron transfer between PCDs and BNNSs [23]. In Fig. 1 (e), a characteristic Raman absorption at 1365 cm−1 is detected in BNNSs. Both PCDs and PCD@BNNSs samples show two characteristic Raman absorptions around 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which correspond to the D band and G band of carbon-based materials [24]. It can be seen that the D band shifts from 1349 cm−1 for PCDs to 1334 cm−1 for PCD@BNNSs, whereas the G band shifts from 1595 cm−1 to 1577 cm−1, respectively. Raman results confirm the π-π interactions between PCDs and BNNSs, which are well agreed with the UV–vis measurements [25]. The XRD patterns of BNNSs, PCDs, and PCD@BNNSs hybrids are shown in Fig. 1(f). A typical 2θ peak around 26.8° is observed for the BNNS sample, which can be assigned to the typical 002 peak of BN crystal lattice [26]. For the PCDs, two characteristic diffraction peaks at 24.2° and 42.8° are found, which can be ascribed to the (002) and (100) plane of amorphous graphitic carbon. In terms of the PCD@BNNSs, the characteristic diffraction peaks both PCDs and BNNSs are detected in PCD@BNNSs. In addition, the d-spacing of the PCD@BNNSs hybrids increases to 20.7 Å, implying the surface absorption of PCDs enlarges the interlayer spacing of BNNSs [27].

In Fig. 2 (a)–(d), SEM, TEM, and AFM are used to investigate the microstructure changes of PCDs, BNNSs, SM, and PCD@BNNSs. The PCDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction between CDs and phosphoric acid. In Fig. 2(a1), SEM image indicates the PCDs with the typical nanoparticle structure. As illustrated in Fig. 2(b1), the TEM result reveals that the as-synthesized PCDs have a size of 10 ∼ 25 nm. The thickness of the PCDs is ca. 2.5 nm, analyzed using the AFM testing in Fig. 2(c1)-(d1). Based on our previous work [16], the large-size ultrathin BNNSs (Fig. S1(b)-(d) and Fig. 2(a2)-(b2)) were prepared through exfoliation from bulk hBN, and the average lateral size of the BNNSs is about 9.52 μm (Fig. S2). The thickness of the exfoliated large-size BNNSs is ca. 3.3 nm, in Fig. 2(c2)-(d2). In general, the theoretical thickness of single-layer BNNS is around 0.5 ∼ 1.0 nm [28], indicating the exfoliated BNNSs with a typical few-layer structure. In Fig. 2(a3) and Fig. S6, the SEM images of PCD@BNNSs reveal the overall decoration of PCDs on the BNNSs. Noteworthy, overall the distribution of PCDs on BNNSs is difficult to view because their smaller size contrast under electron microscopies. To confirm the uniform distribution, TEM testing was further performed for the PCD@BNNSs. In Fig. 2(b3), the TEM shows the PCDs are homogeneously distributed on the thin BNNSs’ surface. Besides, the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern in the inset indicates the hexagonal lattice structure with a dislocation angle of about 13°. In addition, the EDS mappings suggest that the P element is uniformly distributed on the PCD@BNNSs (Fig. S7). As shown in Figs. (c3)-(d3), the AFM images confirm the thickness of PCD@BNNSs is composed of PCDs and BNNSs, in which the thickness BNNSs is ca. 3.4 nm while the PCDs is ca. 6.5 nm. Obviously, the PCDs on BNNSs’ surface are thicker than the single PCD in Fig. 2(d1). This is mainly due to the multiple adsorptions of PCDs on the BNNSs.

In the inset in Fig. 2(b1), the PCDs show good dispersion in DIW, due to they contain a large number of hydrophilic groups (e.g., –COOH, –OH, and -PO3H2, etc). In our method, the highest concentration of PCDs water suspensions can reach up to 20 mg/ml, and keep their stable condition over several months. In fact, the exfoliated BNNSs could be also well dispersed in DIW through simple sonication, due to the introduction of –OH groups (see Fig. S1(e)). Unfortunately, owing to the stronger interactions between large-size BNNSs layers, the dispersion exhibited typical restacking and aggregation behaviors after a long-term storage (inset in Fig. 2(b2)). In contrast, the PCD@BNNSs behave superior dispersion capability in liquids. In Fig. 2(b3) the inset, the PCD@BNNSs water suspension with a concentration of ca. 5 mg/ml exhibits stable dispersion state without any aggregation phenomenon. This is mainly attributed to the following reasons: (1) the hydrophilic groups of PCDs facilitate the dispersion capability of PCD@BNNSs; (2) the PCDs act as spacers can restrict the fast restacking and aggregation of BNNSs. All in all, our approach is able to obtain high-quality BNNSs dispersions, which is much benefited to offer longer, more tortuous pathways for hindering ions fast penetration.

The CDs have been reported to provide good corrosion inhibition effect for metals in acid solutions, owing to its high affinity with various metallic ions (e.g. Fe3+, Fe2+) through the strong absorption function from surface –OH, –COOH, –NH2, etc [29,30]. In our previous works [31], it was confirmed that the phosphate groups could act with the metal surface to form hydrophobic P-O-M bonds in neutral water solution, and create passivation protection layers. Furthermore, XPS survey demonstrates the coexistence of –OH, –COOH, and -PO3H2 groups in PCD@BNNSs (Fig. 3 (a)-(c)). In view of these, thus the PCDs are expected to service as excellent corrosion inhibitor. To validate the corrosion inhibition effect, the EIS plots of Q235 steel electrodes were recorded after 24 h of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) without and with 100 mg/l of PCDs and PCD@BNNSs, in a three electrode system (Fig. S8(a)). Fig. 3(d)-(f) show the Nyquist and Bode plots based on the EIS tests, the results were fitted using ZSimpWin software based on the electrical equivalent circuits (Figs. S8(b) and S8(c)). From the EIS results in Fig. 3(d)-(f), the capacitive loops, the lowest-frequency impedance modulus (Z0.01, 1001 O cm2) value, and the breakpoint frequency (f b, 0.24 Hz) of the steel electrode soaked in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) with 100 mg/l of PCD@BNNSs are higher, larger, and lower than those immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) with 100 mg/l of CDs (Z0.01 = 820 O cm2, f b = 0.32 Hz) and pure 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) (Z0.01 = 547 O cm2, f b = 0.40 Hz), respectively. Moreover, the fitted results (Fig. 3(e)) show that the polarization resistance (Rc = 1162 O cm2) of PCDs@BNNS solution is much higher than those of PCD (Rc = 1096 O cm2) solution and pure 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) (Rc = 701 O cm2), respectively. These results imply that the PCD@BNNSs can protect the steel electrode from aggressive species in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq).

The corrosion behavior of the steel electrodes was further investigated through Tafel polarization technique after 24 h of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) without and with 100 mg/l of PCDs and PCD@BNNSs. According to the polarization plots in Fig. 3(g), the corrosion current (i corr) can be obtained through a polarization extrapolation method [32]. It can be observed that both cathodic and anodic i corr values become smaller after loading PCDs@BNNSs, revealing the PCDs@BNNSs can suppress both cathode (reduction of oxygen) and anode (dissolution of Fe) corrosion reactions (Fig. S9). As illustrated in Fig. 3(h), the icorr of PCD@BNNSs (0.58 μA/cm2) is 1 ∼ 2 orders of magnitude lower than those of PCDs (1.3 μA/cm2) and pure (12 μA/cm2) NaCl(aq). In addition, the corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) can be calculated by the following [33]:

are the corrosion current of steel electrodes immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) without and with 100 mg/l of corrosion inhibitors. Based on the i corr, the IE values of PCDs and PCD@BNNSs are ca. 89% and ca. 95%, respectively, both of which are higher than that of NCDs (ca. 80.8%) previously reported [21]. The absorption and corrosion inhibition mechanisms of PCDs and PCD@BNNSs are shown in Fig. 3(i). This is because of the fact that the PCDs can anchor on the steel surface generate a stable passivation layer via chelations between the O and P heteroatoms with metal ions, the hydrophilic phosphate ester groups can be transferred into hydrophobic P-O-Fe groups, and large-size BNNSs further block the fast transfer of aggressive species, and thus decline the corrosion rate and corrosion probability of the steel substrates [34,35]. Fig. 3(j)-(l) display the surface profiles of steel surfaces after immersed in different solution systems. In Fig. 3(j), the steel substrate immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) for 24 h has a relatively large surface roughness, indicating it suffered from serious corrosion. After adding PCDs in Fig. 3(k), the corrosion degree of the steel substrate is greatly reduced. Especially for the PCD@BNNSs sample, no obvious corrosion traces can be observed on the steel surface in Fig. 3(l). This result is well consist with the EIS and Tafel plots.

The dispersion state of BNNSs plays a crucial role in the barrier and corrosion resistance of OCCs [36]. The large-size BNNSs are more effective on enhancing the coatings’ performance, but also face problems of difficult dispersion and easy agglomeration in coating matrix. Our PCD@BNNSs show excellent dispersion capability and the –OH and –COOH groups can enhance the interfacial interactions with coating matrix [20,21]. Fig. 4 (a)-(c) display the Ramam mapping results of EP, BNNS/EP, and PCD@BNNS/EP coatings, which enable us to verify the distribution states of BNNSs at overall scales. For the pure EP sample, no signal of BNNSs characteristic Raman absorption is detected (Fig. 4(a)). In contrast, the strong Raman activity of BNNSs in the BNNS/EP and PCD@BNNS/EP composites are observed in Fig. 4(b) and (c). In addition, compared to the BNNS/EP sample, the PCD@BNNS/EP coating has more uniform distribution of Raman mapping. This suggests that the PCDs can facilitate the homogeneous dispersion of large-size BNNSs in the EP matrix.

To further study the dispersion states of BNNSs, the cross-section SEM images of BNNS/EP and PCD@BNNS/EP composites were obtained, as depicted in Fig. 4(d) and (e). It can be seen that, the larger-size BNNSs present parallelly aligned structures in the BNNS/EP and PCD@BNNS/EP systems, confirming their long-range ordered features. However, due to the poor dispersion ability and the strong interlayer interactions of BNNSs, and the essential incompatibility between BNNSs and the EP matrix, the unmodified BNNSs exhibit obviously restacking, aggregation, and debonding with coating matrix in BNNS/EP system (Fig. 4(d) and Figs. S10(a)-(b)). In contrast, PCD@BNNSs are highly ordered alignment and effectively combine with the coating matrix in the PCD@BNNS/EP composite, as shown in Fig. 4(e) and Figs. S10(c)-(d). The oriented degree of BNNSs can be further evidenced using XRD measures. In Fig. 4(f), both BNNS/EP and PCD@BNNS/EP coatings show the two characteristic peaks at 18.5° and 26.5° along the horizontal and vertical directions. Meng et al. confirmed that that the intensity of BNNSs diffraction peak satisfied the Bragg’s equation around 2θ = 26.4° [37], and was proportional to the concentration of 002 planes. Typically, the higher concentration of 002 planes reflects the stronger diffraction peak intensity. When the BNNSs are disordered orientation, few 002 planes of BN support the Bragg’s equation, leading to a weak diffraction peak. However, when the BNNSs are ordered alignment perpendicular to the diffraction direction, most of the 002 planes of BN satisfy the Bragg’s equation [16,38], thus stronger diffraction intensity can be detected. Obviously, the PCD@BNNS/EP coating displays a stronger 002 peak at 26.5◦ along the horizontal direction than that of BNNS/EP, while a weaker 002 peak along the vertical direction than that of of BNNS/EP, revealing that the higher level parallel alignment of BNNSs has been realized in the PCD@BNNS/EP coating, which is well agreed with the Raman and SEM results. This can greatly increase the physical shielding efficiency of the EP matrix during the immersion, and thus enhance the anticorrosion performance of the coating.

By mixing large-size BNNSs in the EP matrix, the composites exhibit high optical transparency and the flowers beneath the films can be seen clearly (Fig. 4(g)–(i)). The transmittance of the PCD@BNNS/EP composite is up to 88% at the visible light region (400–800 nm), that is close to the pure EP (90%, in Fig. 4(j)), suggesting its high transparency. The high transparency is benefited from the good dispersion, ordered alignment, and high interface compatibility of PCD@BNNSs in the EP matrix. For the BNNS/EP, the transmittance (83%) is slightly lower than that of PCD@BNNS/EP due to the poor distribution and interface compatibility of BNNSs. These results are well agreed with the morphology tests. The OTR and WVTR results of the composites are presented in Fig. 4 (k) and 4(l). Compared to EP and BNNS/EP films, the highly dense and self-aligned PCD@BNNSs make the PCD@BNNS/EP film has relatively high gas barrier performance. Especially, water vapor transport is sensitive to both micropores and defects in the films. Owing to the directional ordered architecture which declines the content of micropores and defects, the OTR and WVTR values of the PCD@BNNS/EP composite films reduce by 60% and 27%, respectively, compared to pure EP film, enable it can be served as the protecting coatings for metallic corrosion.

The corrosion behavior of the steel electrodes protected by EP, BNNS/EP, and PCD@BNNS coatings were evaluated by EIS measurements. All coatings were soaked in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) for 5 weeks. Prior of the EIS tests, the OCP of the steel electrodes protected by different were recorded during the 5 weeks of immersion, and the detailed parameters were showed in Fig. S11 and Table S2. Due to the continued deterioration of coatings caused by the infiltration of corrosion medium, the OCP values all of coatings drop with the increase in immersion time. After 5 weeks of immersion, the OCP value of PCD@BNNS/EP is −0.38 V, which is dramatically higher than those of BNNS/EP (−0.62 V) and pure EP (−0.68 V) coatings, suggesting it has the best anticorrosion performance. The Bode-modulus and Bode-phase plots are illustrated in Fig. 5 . For the pure EP coating in Fig. 5(a1) and Fig. 5(d), the Z0.01 value (ca. 3.4 × 109 O cm2) shows rapid decline at the initial immersion. With the immersion time, the Z0.01 tends to be a stable value of ca. 3.2 × 107 O cm2. This reflects that the pure EP coating can provide poor protective capabilities for steel electrode. In Fig. 5(a2), the BNNS/EP sample displays a relatively high Z0.01 value (ca. 3.7 × 109 O cm2) at the initial immersion of 3 weeks due to the high physical barrier property caused by the addition of large-size and self-aligned BNNSs in the coating matrix. However, the Z0.01 sharply reduced to ca. 9.3 × 107 O cm2 after 5 weeks of immersion. This is due to the fact that the aggressive species involve water, oxygen, and chloride ions in the corrosive medium can fast penetration along the coating defects caused by the weak interfacial interactions between BNNSs and EP matrix as well as the restacking of BNNS at the late immersion [38,39]. In Fig. 5(a3), the Z0.01 values of PCD@BNNS/EP coating are consistently as high as ∼ 109 O cm2 throughout the whole 5 week of immersion process, 2 orders of magnitude higher than those of BNNS/EP and pure EP coatings.This can be mainly attributed to the superb physical shielding property and the extra corrosion inhibition effect of PCD@BNNSs. Regardless of the immersion time, the steel electrode under the PCD@BNNS/EP coating has the highest phase angle (Fig. 5 (b3)) compared to the other coatings (Fig. 5(b1) and Fig. 5(b2)), confirming that it provides the best anticorrosion performance for the underlying steel. In addition, the Nyquist plots (Fig. 5(c1)-(c3)) demonstrate that the diameter of the semicircle has an order of PCD@BNNS/EP > BNNS/EP > EP, which is consist with the OCP, Z0.01, and phase angle results. EIS tests reveal that the PCD@BNNS/EP coating has the most superior corrosion resistance performance.

To further investigate the corrosion behaviours of the different coatings protected steels, the EIS results were then fitted by using the corresponding equivalent circuit models (Figs. S8(b)-(c)) [40]. The selection of the equivalent circuit model is in view of the corresponding time constants and the goodness of fits. For a complete coating system (Fig. S8(b)), the equivalent circuit model often has R c and coating capacitance (Q c) [41]. For a damaged coating system (Fig. S8(c)), the equivalent circuit model usually contains extra charge transfer resistance (Rct) and double layered capacitance (Qdl) [42]. Besides, Rs represents the solution resistance, and its contribution to the circuit is negligible compared with other parameters [43]. The detailed electrochemical parameters were summarized in Table S2. Generally, Rc is regarded as an important factor to assess the anticorrosion performance of coatings [44]. A larger Rc value reflects that the aggressive species are more difficult to permeate into the coating matrix [45]. In Fig. 5(e), the Rc value of EP sharply declines from 3.0 × 109 O cm2 to 4.2 × 106 O cm2 during the entire immersion time. This reveals that the pure EP coating has been almost penetrated by aggressive corrosive components, and the steel electrode was severely corroded. Similar to the Z0.01 value, the BNNS/EP shows a large Rc value of 3.0 × 109 O cm2 during the initial 3 weeks of immersion, however, which fast declines to 8.0 × 107 O cm2 after 5 weeks of immersion. On contrary, after the 5 weeks of immersion, the PCD@BNNS/EP remains a high Rc value of 6.3 × 109 O cm2, which is 2 ∼ 3 orders of magnitude higher than those of BNNS/EP and pure EP coatings. This further confirms that the addition PCD@BNNSs significantly increased the impermeability and anticorrosion performance of EP coatings. Rc results are well agreed with the analysis of OCP and Z0.01 tests. Fig. 5(f) behaves the dependencies of f b values with the immersion time. After the 5 weeks of immersion, the f b value of EP dramatically increases from 0.78 Hz to 54.9 Hz, suggesting that the fast water penetration led to the serious deterioration and failure of the coating matrix (inset in Fig. 5(f)). For the BNNS/EP, the f b increased fractionally from 0.5 Hz to 9.5 Hz during the 5 weeks of immersion, indicating that the steel electrode suffers from marginally corrosion (inset in Fig. 5(f)). In terms of the PCD@BNNS/EP, the f b shows a fluctuation around 0.22 Hz during the whole immersion time, which suggests that PCD@BNNS/EP greatly delays the diffusion of aggressive species and blocks the detachment of coatings from substrates (inset in Fig. 5(f)). This benefited from the exceptional physical shielding function and passivation effects of the highly self-aligned and strong interfacial adhesion PCD@BNNSs hybrids. For these reasons, the PCD@BNNS/EP coating demonstrates outstanding long-term corrosion resistance performance, making it singled out from the previous related 2D-based OCCs researches [7,8,10,11,23,25,39,43,46–48] (See Table S3 for details).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of corrosion process and Mechanism.
ce:section.ce:para: After immersion, the coatings were exfoliated from the steel surfaces for SEM tests. In Fig. 6 (a), the pure EP coating matrix has been completely penetrated by the corrosion aggressive species during the immersion, indicating that pure EP coating has a poor barrier and anticorrosion performance. Some penetration pits are observed on the BNNS/EP coating surface, while no obvious aggressive species are detected in the regions, in Fig. 6(b). This demonstrates that the addition of large-size BNNSs could reduce the penetration rate of corrosion medium. For the PCD@BNNS/EP sample in Fig. 6(c), very few defects are found on its surface, suggesting its superior barrier performance. To interpret the corrosion behaviours of the steel electrodes and confirm the significance of the PCD@BNNSs against corrosion, Raman and SEM measurements were carried out to analyse the corrosion products of steel surfaces after 5 weeks of immersion. The corrosion products of steel surfaces were analysed by Raman characterizations. In Fig. 6(d), the EP sample shows typical Raman activity, implying the steel has undergone serious corrosion reaction, and corrosion products are mainly composed of FeOOH and few Fe3O4 (Eqs. S1-S8) [43]. In Fig. 6(e), similar Raman activity is detected in BNNS/EP sample, however, the corrosion products consist mainly of Fe3O4, and the intensity of FeOOH is lower than that of EP system, indicating the steel is in the passivation state [46]. As expected, due to the diffusion channels to ionic flow in the coating were prominently prolong and better corrosion inhibition effects, corrosion products could not be detected on the PCD@BNNS/EP coating protected steel surface, In Fig. 6(f). This again reveals that the PCD@BNNS/EP coating has the most outstanding anticorrosion performance. Fig. 6(g)-(i) present the SEM images of the steel surfaces protected by different coatings. In Fig. 6(g), the steel surface protected by pure EP coating is completely covered by corrosion products, revealing the steel was suffered from serious corrosion during the immersion. After addition of BNNSs, the corrosion products on the BNNS/EP sample coated steel surface decreased in Fig. 6(h), indicating most of the corrosion reactions of steels were effectively suppressed due to the increased barrier property. For the PCD@BNNS/EP system in Fig. 6(i), the steel surface is smooth without visible corrosion products even at the magnification level of 10, 000 ×. This indicates that the aggressive species just contacted to the steel surface during the 5 weeks of immersion, yet has not caused the coating failure. SEM results further demonstrate that the PCD@BNNS/EP coating possessess the best anticorrosion performance, which is agreed with electrochemical and Raman tests.

To confirm the long-term corrosion protection of the PCD@BNNS/EP coating for industrial applications, neutral salt spray measurements were conducted via spraying a 5.0 wt% NaCl fog at 35 °C. Before testing, an artificial scratch was made on the coating surfaces by using a scalpel. Fig. 6(j)-(l) display the digital photos of EP, BNNS/EP, and PCD@BNNS/EP coating/steel specimens before and after exposure for 200 h and 500 h in a salt fog-box. In Fig. 6(j), the scratch of the EP sample was almost fully filled by rust, and blisters present on the coating surface, suggesting that the aggressive species had reached the steel surface and the coating matrix began to separation from substrate. For the BNNS/EP in Fig. 6(k), the serious corrosion appeared near the scratch, but the coating matrix still keeps complete, indicating that the corrosion degree of the BNNS/EP system was reduced. This is because that the addition of large-size BNNSs enhanced the shielding effects and corrosion protection performance of the coating. Surprisingly, after 500 h of salt spray tests, the PCD@BNNS/EP features high surface integrity in Fig. 6 (l), just trace rust is observed around the scratch, revealing the PCD@BNNS/EP coating has the best anticorrosion performance. This is due to that highly self-aligned and dense large-size BNNSs could form longer tortuous nanochannels and fill the defects of coating matrix, which is more benefit to exert the physical shielding effects. Besides, the PCDs could provide the extra passivation function to further reduce the corrosion rate. Salt spray tests are good consistent with the electrochemical results.

To understand the failure process and provide theoretical support for further investigation, clarifying the shielding mechanism of the PCD@BNNS/EP coating is very necessary [45]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the protection mechanism of the PCD@BNNS/EP coating can be explained as the follows: (1) the highly-parallelly aligned large-size BNNSs can form longer tortuous 2D nanochannels, tremendously block the infiltration of the aggressive species and thus prolong the service life of coating matrix; (2) the PCD@BNNS/EP composite coating is designed to feature strong interfacial interactions, effectively retards the propagation of cracks and make the coating more durable, thus reduces the corrosion probability of the steel substrate; (3) the PCDs can form stable P-O-Fe passivation layers through chelating on steel surface to arrest the further oxidation of FeOOH, enabling the substrate keeps a passivation state to profoundly reduce the corrosion rate (Eqs. S9-S12). Based on these above advantages, the PCD@BNNS/EP coating can prevent the steel from fast corrosion in corrosion medium, contributing to the outstanding long-term corrosion resistance performance.

The pivotal virtue of our approach is the simultaneous realization of superior dispersion and corrosion inhibition of PCD@BNNSs hybrids, enabling the PCD@BNNS/EP composite coatings with outstanding long-term anticorrosion performance. The PCDs were used as a modifier of large-size ultrathin BNNSs, which significantly promoted the uniform distribution of BNNSs in the coating and strong interfacial interactions with the EP matrix. After immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl(aq) for 5 weeks, the coating resistance of the PCD@BNNS/EP coating was up to 6.3 × 109 O cm2, which is 2 ∼ 3 orders of magnitude higher than those of BNNS/EP and pure EP coatings. The interfacial protection mechanism of the PCD@BNNS/EP coating was revealed in detail. This is ascribed to the fact that highly self-aligned and dense PCD@BNNSs served as excellent sealing walls effectively prevent the fast erosion of aggressive species. In addition, the PCDs could provide extra passivation function form through forming P-O-Fe protective layers, which immensely blocked the continuous deterioration of the interfaces. Our work opens a unique avenue for the design and fabrication of functional fillers for high-performance anticorrosion coatings. On the one hand, it can address the aggregation problem of large-size 2D nanosheets; on the other hand, we introduce an extra passivation mechanism that can achieve the long-term protection for metals.
